coredata.dc:title: Anchoring silver nanoparticles on graphene quantum dots: A highly efficient, green, and rapid nano-catalyst for the reduction of nitro compounds and tandem reductive Ugi reactions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids

coredata.dc:description:
In this work, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were successfully anchored on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The resulting GQDs/AgNPs were then applied as a nano-catalyst for the reduction of nitro compounds, after confirming their stability at various pH. For this purpose, the reduction of nitrobenzene (NB) was selected as a model reaction in the presence of sodium borohydride (NaBH4) under green conditions and controlled by UV–vis spectroscopy. The kinetic of the reaction was investigated and found that the reaction followed a pseudo-first-order kinetic model. Interestingly, further studies showed that a minimum amount of the catalyst (1 μg) exhibited excellent efficiency and achieved a high reduction rate. (observed rate constant, k
obs = 2.5 × 10−3 s−1 and normalized rate constant, k
nor = 2500 s−1mg−1). Additionally, the catalytic activity of the resulting GQDs/AgNPs was examined to promote the reduction reaction of NB derivatives and the tandem Ugi reactions. The products of both reactions were prepared in good to high yields (80–94 %). The outstanding advantages of the present catalyst include its green protocol, eco-friendly, inexpensive, rapid reaction, mild reaction conditions, and operational simplicity.

head.ce:title: Anchoring silver nanoparticles on graphene quantum dots: A highly efficient, green, and rapid nano-catalyst for the reduction of nitro compounds and tandem reductive Ugi reactions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic Chemistry, Shahid Beheshti University, Daneshjou Boulevard, Tehran, 1983969411, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic Chemistry, Shahid Beheshti University, Daneshjou Boulevard, Tehran, 1983969411, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this work, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were successfully anchored on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The resulting GQDs/AgNPs were then applied as a nano-catalyst for the reduction of nitro compounds, after confirming their stability at various pH. For this purpose, the reduction of nitrobenzene (NB) was selected as a model reaction in the presence of sodium borohydride (NaBH4) under green conditions and controlled by UV–vis spectroscopy. The kinetic of the reaction was investigated and found that the reaction followed a pseudo-first-order kinetic model. Interestingly, further studies showed that a minimum amount of the catalyst (1 μg) exhibited excellent efficiency and achieved a high reduction rate. (observed rate constant, k
obs = 2.5 × 10−3 s−1 and normalized rate constant, k
nor = 2500 s−1mg−1). Additionally, the catalytic activity of the resulting GQDs/AgNPs was examined to promote the reduction reaction of NB derivatives and the tandem Ugi reactions. The products of both reactions were prepared in good to high yields (80–94 %). The outstanding advantages of the present catalyst include its green protocol, eco-friendly, inexpensive, rapid reaction, mild reaction conditions, and operational simplicity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The term "quantum dots" was first used by Mark Reed in 1986, and in 2023, the Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded to Moungi G. Bawendi, Louis E. Brus, and Alexei I. Ekimov for their groundbreaking work in the discovery and synthesis of these nanomaterials. Nowadays, quantum dots (QDs) are recognized as pivotal components in nanotechnology and nanoscience, with amazing applications in various fields [1]. For instance, Guo and Chu have developed highly effective catalysts that exploit the unique properties of QDs, highlighting their significance in advancing nano-catalytic systems [2–4]. On the other hand, since 2010, when Geim and Novoselov received the Nobel Prize in Physics for their work in the field of graphene [5], graphene and graphene-based materials have attracted more attention from scientists [6]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a kind of graphene-based carbon nanomaterial, have recently obtained much interest, alongside carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and carbon nanotubes [7]. This interest is largely due to their remarkable properties, including water solubility, low toxicity, bio-compatibility, and high photostability [8]. Till now, two main approaches, “bottom-up” [9] and “top-down” [10], have been introduced for the preparation of GQDs containing graphene fragments with a lateral dimension of <20 nm [11]. Among a variety of carbon source materials for the preparation of GQDs, bio-precursors such as citric acid (CA) have become more popular due to their advantageous characteristics [12].

CA-based GQDs have favorable properties such as aqueous solubility, eco-friendly nature, abundance, diverse functionality, sustainability, and bio-compatibility [13]. Although numerous applications such as biomedical, bioimagings, drug carrier, photodetectors, solar cells, sensors, batteries, fluorescent agents, and light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [14] have been reported for GQDs in recent years, less consideration has been paid to their use as catalysts or catalyst supports [15]. This study seeks to bridge this gap by exploring the synergistic effects of GQDs in catalysis and their interactions with metal nanoparticles (MNPs). Many active sites on the surface of citric acid-based GQDs, such as carboxylic functional groups, make them efficient inhibitors of various metal ions or nanoparticles (NPs) [16]. They can effectively complex with metal ions or NPs, preventing their leaching into water solutions [17]. It has been proven that the synergistic effect in GQDs alone and the metal-supported GQDs greatly increase the activity and stability of the catalysts [18]. The remarkable affinity between the carbon surface in the GQDs and the MNPs was illustrated by Li et al., which confirmed the immobilization of MNPs using covalent interactions with graphene [19]. Indeed, they laid the groundwork for subsequent studies in this field. In addition, metal-supported GQDs were used as photocatalysis [20], electrocatalysis [21], photoelectrocatalysis [22], and chemical catalysis [23] in chemical transformations. In recent years, chemical catalysts have become an attractive research field for scientists, so several reports have been published in this series.

In the past few decades, MNPs have been utilized in various fields, including biology, medicine, biomedicine, sensing, optics, and especially catalysts, due to their remarkable potential and properties [24]. Recently, the use of precious nanoparticles (NPs) such as Au, Pd, Pt, and Ag-based has been greatly promoted [25]. In particular, AgNPs have attracted particular attention due to their special physical, chemical, and optical properties, and their cost-effectiveness [26]. Among the various applications of AgNPs [27], using them as catalysts has drawn great interest from organic chemists [28,29]. However, challenges such as aggregation, poor stability, and easy oxidation of naked AgNPs are among the reasons that reduce the catalytic effect of these NPs [30]. The most efficient solution for this problem is the immobilization of NPs on supporting material [31].

Aniline, the simplest aromatic amine, was first synthesized by dry distillation of indigo in 1826. In the following, the same oily compound was generated by heating indigo with potash, and in 1840, it was named aniline. In 1843, the structure of aniline was known by its preparation from the reduction of nitrobenzene (NB) [32]. The literature surveys show that aromatic amines are very beneficial chemicals for antipyretic, analgesic, anticorrosive additives, and photographic developers [33]. On the other hand, NB compounds are highly hazardous pollutants that are abundantly found in agricultural and industrial wastewater [34]. Due to the toxicity and carcinogenicity of NBs, they were categorized as one of 129 prominent pollutants for humans and the environment by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA, USA) [35]. Therefore, the conversion of harmful nitro compounds into valuable aromatic amines in laboratory and industrial scale is of great importance [36]. Recent studies have highlighted the effectiveness of MNPs, particularly AgNPs, in facilitating this transformation using sodium borohydride (NaBH4) as a reducing agent [37]. To improve green conditions, the use of AgNPs-based catalyst converters with higher activities of silver atoms was significantly utilized for the nitro reduction reactions in recent years [38]. Accordingly, employing the GQDs as an efficient support [39] can be the basis for constructing a powerful catalytic system for this purpose.

A tandem reaction, also known as a cascade reaction or domino reaction, is a chemical shortcut that involves two or more sequential reactions such that the products of previous reactions can instantly participate in the next reactions [40]. The tandem strategy introduces an efficient method for synthesizing important structures from inexpensive, unexpansive, and readily available starting materials in a relatively rapid reaction [41]. Also, the tandem reactions as simple and green processes have higher atom economy and safety than the corresponding traditional reactions [42]. Reductive and oxidative tandem reactions are the most common types of tandem reactions. In these reactions, one of the intermediates or starting materials is reduced or oxidized and the created functional group participates in the next reaction [43]. Notably, the nitro reduction reaction as part of tandem reactions has become a targeted strategy for the synthesis of valuable compounds [43,44].

Inspired by the aforementioned explanations, this article presents the design and preparation of a robust catalytic system composed of GQDs/AgNPs. This system was developed using a simple, clean, eco-friendly, and green method that involves anchoring AgNPs on the surface of GQDs. The catalytic activity of the synthesized GQDs/AgNPs was examined in nitro reduction reactions and tandem Ugi reactions using NaBH4 as a reducing agent in water. The aim of this research is to investigate the potential of CA-based GQDs as effective supports for AgNPs, focusing on their ability to enhance catalytic activity and stability. The findings are anticipated to provide valuable insights for the development of more efficient catalytic systems aimed at sustainable chemical transformations.

All the initial materials including citric acid monohydrate (CA, 98 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98 %), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99 %), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 99.99 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %), nitrobenzenes (nitrobenzene (99 %), 2-nitrotoluol (99 %), 1-ethyl-2-nitrobenzene (99 %), 1-chloro-4-nitrobenzene (96 %), 2-chlor-6-nitrotoluol (99 %), 3-chlor-4-fluornitrobenzol (98 %), and 3-nitroanisol (99 %)), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 98 %), benzaldehyde (99 %), carboxylic acids (4-methylbenzoic acid (98 %) and 3-methylbenzoic acid (99 %)), and isocyanides (cyclohexyl isocyanide (98 %), isopropyl isocyanide (97 %), and tert-butyl isocyanide (98 %)) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Merck Chemicals Co. (Germany) and utilized without any purification.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, model 1700, Japan) under ambient, basic, and acidic pH conditions. The pH of samples was adjusted with NaOH and HCl aqueous solutions and measured on a Metrohm 827 pH lab, 230 V, EU with Primatrode (Switzerland). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples were received using a spectrofluorometric (PerkinElmer, LS45, Germany). Attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) was made by an FT–IR spectrometer equipped with ATR (Thermo Nicolet, model iS50, USA). Transmission electron micrograph (TEM) images were attained using an electron microscope (EM 900, Carl Zeiss, Germany). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) armed with Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) device (TESCAN, Mira 3-XMU, Czech) was used to characterize EDX and the surface morphology of the samples. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) followed by zeta potential measurements were ascertained with a DLSZP/Particle Sizer (Malvern, MAL1032660, England). Powder X‐ray diffraction (XRD) information was collected at room temperature in the 10–80 2θ range using a STOE STADI P diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. The concentrations of silver were calculated by an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES; Varian Vista PRO Radial). In addition, 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra of products were obtained in CDCl3 using the Avance spectrometer (Bruker DRX-30072, Germany).

According to the previous reports [45], the GQDs were prepared from direct pyrolysis of CA. In a usual procedure, 2 g CA was poured into a 5 mL beaker and heated to 200 °C using a laboratory furnace. After 30 min, the obtained orange liquid was added drop by drop into 100 mL of 10 mg mL−1 NaOH solution under vigorous stirring. After neutralization, the 2 % stock solution of GQDs was prepared and stored in the dark refrigerator for later use.

GQDs/AgNPs were prepared according to the known method [46]. Briefly, 2.5 mL of GQDs solution was taken and made to a volume of 100 mL. Tollen's reagent was freshly prepared by the addition of NH4OH (28 %) into 1 mL of AgNO3 (50 mg mL−1) solution dropwise to form Ag(NH3)2OH. The above two solutions were fully mixed under vigorous stirring for 60 min at 100 °C. Finally, a purplish red GQDs/AgNPs solution was obtained. For further accessibility, the prepared catalyst was reduced to black powder by a freeze-drying.

The catalytic efficiency of GQDs/AgNPs was initially investigated for the reduction of NB. In follow-up catalyst evaluation, the potential of GQDs/AgNPs was explored for the tandem reductive Ugi reactions. The catalytic activity tests are as follows.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for model nitro reduction reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
The catalytic performance of the prepared GQDs/AgNPs was examined by the reduction of toxic NB to aniline. For this purpose, GQDs/AgNPs (1 μg) were added to the mixture containing distilled water (1800 μL), NB (25 μL, 10 mM), and NaBH4 (200 μL, 10 mM) in a standard quartz cell (3 mL, path length: 1 cm). Then, the cell was still put in a UV–vis spectrometer, and spectra were sequentially scanned every 60 s for 15 min. All spectra were recorded in 200–500 nm wavelength range at ambient temperature and atmospheric pressure. To obtain reaction conversion efficiency, UV–vis spectra of NB were recorded at different standard concentrations. After that, absorbance at λmax = 265 nm was plotted against different standard concentrations, resulting in the derivation of Eq. (1) and Eq. (2).
Eq. (1)

where A, C, a, and b are the UV–vis absorbance at λmax = 265 nm, the concentration of the NB, slope, and intercept of the curve, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for reduction of NB derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: According to the excellent results of the optimized model reaction, NB derivatives were also tested for nitro reduction reactions. Generally, NB derivatives were reduced with the following method:

In a test tube, the desired amount of GQDs/AgNPs (4 mg) was added to the mixture containing distilled water (2 mL), NBs (1 mmol) and NaBH4 (8 mmol, 303 mg). Afterward, the progress of the reactions was checked using thin-layer chromatography (TLC) for 15 min. When the products were observed, the crude mixtures were extracted by ethyl acetate (EtOAc). The products were purified using a plate with EtOAc: n-hexane solvent, after removing the solvents and loading the resulting mixtures on silica gel. Finally, all the purified products were authenticated by 1H and 13C NMR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for tandem reductive Ugi reactions
ce:section.ce:para: As a second part of the catalytic tests, the catalytic approach of GQDs/AgNPs was studied in the tandem reductive Ugi reactions to prepare pseudo-dipeptide compounds. For this aim, GQDs/AgNPs (4 mg) were added to the container reaction containing a mixture of distilled water (3 mL), NB derivative (1.5 mmol), and NaBH4 (8 mmol, 303 mg) and stirred for 10 min at room temperature. Next, benzaldehyde (1 mmol, 106 mg), carboxylic acids (1 mmol), and an isocyanide (1 mmol) were added to the reaction container and stirred for 30 min at room temperature. Finally, the products were collected by filtration and washed with n-hexane to prepare a pure precipitate.

The special applications of GQDs 2 have attracted the attention of many scientists and caused the publication of numerous reports in this field. For example, in our previous works, GQDs-based nanomaterials were reported as compounds with excellent photoluminescent, antibacterial, drug carrier, and sorbent properties [45,47–49]. Herein, we described the synthesis of GQDs/AgNPs 3 as bio-based nanomaterials with excellent catalytic properties. For this purpose, the GQDs 2 (light yellow solution) were initially synthesized from direct pyrolysis of CA 1. In the second step, the GQDs/AgNPs 3 (purplish red solution) were prepared through the reaction between the diluted solution of GQDs 2 and Tollen's reagent at 100 °C for 60 min. Finally, the synthesized catalyst was transformed into a black powder using the freeze-dryer process to increase accessibility. (Scheme 1 ). The catalytic activity of the prepared GQDs/AgNPs 3 was evaluated through nitro reduction reactions and tandem Ugi reactions. In the following sections, the properties and applications of the synthesized catalyst will be discussed in detail.

Fig. 1 displays the ATR-IR spectra of CA, GQDs, and GQDs/AgNPs. In the spectrum of GQDs compared to CA, distinct peaks are observed at around 1384 cm−1 and 1558 cm−1, which is related to symmetric and asymmetric -COO- stretching vibrations. Also, the absorption peak at 2985 cm−1 along with a broad peak in the range of 3100–3500 cm−1 is attributed to C–H and O–H stretching vibrations, respectively, proposing that the GQDs contain some incompletely carbonized CA [45,50]. In the spectrum of GQDs/AgNPs, the intensity of the -COO- peaks (around 1384 cm−1 and 1558 cm−1) has decreased after interaction with Ag+. In addition, the vibration band around 1384 cm−1 was shifted to a lower region (around 1326 cm−1) after forming GQDs/AgNPs. Also, the peaks corresponding to C–H stretching vibrations (at 2985 cm) and O–H stretching vibrations (in the range of 3100–3500 cm-1) are absent in the GQDs/AgNPs spectrum. Above all others, the Ag–O vibration peak around 695 cm−1 was observed, indicating the successful anchoring of Ag+ ions on the GQDs and confirming the successful synthesis of GQDs/AgNPs.

UV–vis and PL spectra are useful documents for identifying the optical and luminescent features of the formed nanocomposites, respectively. Fig. 2 a illustrates the UV–vis spectra of the GQDs and GQDs/AgNPs in distilled water. The absorption spectrum of GQDs exhibits two peaks at 250 and 360 nm, which are attributed to the π–π∗ and n–π∗ transitions, respectively. Additionally, a long tail extending into the visible region was seen for the GQDs, which is in agreement with previous reports [47]. After anchoring the AgNPs, the absorption peaks of GQDs weakened. In contrast, a narrow absorption peak appeared at about 405 nm, allocated to the surface plasmon absorption of the AgNPs (a reduced form of Ag+ ions) [51,52]. The decrease in the surface plasmon resonance wavelength (λSPR) in the GQDs/AgNPs spectrum is attributed to the redshift that happens with the increase in the nanoparticle size following anchoring [53]. In the follow-up identifying the prepared nanomaterials, the PL spectra of excitation wavelength-dependent related to GQDs comparison with GQDs/AgNPs are shown in Fig. 2b. Due to the planar structure of GQDs and the uniform size of sp2 clusters of them, the emission wavelength of GQDs is almost independent of excitement. Accordingly, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths are recorded at 362 and 460 nm, respectively [54]. Therefore, blue light (444 nm) was emitted by the solution of GQDs when excited with a 360 nm UV beam. The interaction between GQDs and AgNPs was ratified by quenching the PL activity of GQDs in GQDs/AgNPs.

The crystalline natures of both GQDs and GQDs/AgNPs were investigated by the XRD technique. As shown in Fig. 3 , only a weak wide peak was discovered in the XRD pattern of GQDs, which is proper evidence for the carbonizing of CA to obtain graphene structures containing abundant functional groups [55], consistent with previous reports on the synthesis of GQDs from CA [54]. In contrast, the XRD pattern of GQDs/AgNPs shows peaks at 29.44°, 38.20°, 44.56°, 64.60°, and 77.62° in the 2θ range of 10–80. The peaks at 38.20°, 44.56°, 64.60°, and 77.62° correspond to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of silver, respectively, as confirmed by the JCPDS file number 04–0783 which corresponds to the FCC structure of silver [56]. It should be mentioned that, the peak at 29.44° may be attributed to other crystalline structures or impurities, and further investigation is required to determine its exact origin [57]. However, these sharp peaks clearly indicate the successful formation of GQDs/AgNPs.

Scrutiny of the morphology of the samples along with their comparison provides valuable information. As shown in Fig. 4 a–d, FE-SEM analysis was used to study the morphologies of GQDs/AgNPs. FE-SEM images of GQDs/AgNPs indicated a monotonic circular configuration. The increase in particle size (from the range of 4–6 nm to about 22.46 nm) along with the change in the zeta potential (from −0.684 mV to +0.165 mV) after anchoring AgNPs (a reduced state of Ag + ions) on GQDs evidenced the formation of the GQDs/AgNPs. On the other hand, the TEM images displayed modified GQDs by AgNPs, which clearly confirmed the formation of GQDs/AgNPs (Fig. 4e and f). Although the distribution of AgNPs appears non-uniform, this aspect could be influenced by factors such as synthesis conditions and interactions between AgNPs and GQDs.

The high anchoring ability of AgNPs of GQDs compared to pure GQDs was deduced by mapping, EDX, and ICP‐OES analysis. Elemental mapping results of GQDs/AgNPs persuasively demonstrated that silver was dispersed monotonically in structure (Fig. 5 ). Significantly, the presence of this element was supported by EDX results (Fig. 5). In addition, the quantitative determination of the silver content in the catalyst was accomplished by the ICP‐OES analysis. The results disclosed 318 mmol/g silver loading in the GQDs/AgNPs as a catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of GQDs/AgNPs under different pH
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 illustrates the UV–visible spectra of the GQDs/AgNPs across a wide range of pH values. As the pH of the medium changed from 2 to 12, the surface plasmon resonance wavelength (λSPR) exhibited a slight red shift from 401.5 to 406. On the other hand, increasing in pH of the environment resulted in a slight decrease in the absorption of the plasmon band. The above evidence proved that these nanoparticles are stable under various pH conditions.

One of the main factors in the stability of AgNPs under different pH is the strong interaction between these nanoparticles and -COO- groups present on the substrates [58]. In fact, it is possible that the -COO- groups are protonated at low pH; consequently, the resulting –COOH groups cannot effectively stabilize the AgNPs and these nanoparticles rapidly aggregate. In this aggregated state, the surface charge is concentrated over a small area, causing electrons to oscillate at high speed and couple with electromagnetic radiation of high energy and low wavelength. This results in a slight blue shift in λSPR. In contrast, at higher pH, the electrostatic force between -COO- groups increases the distance between AgNPs. Finally, the λSPR is slightly red-shifted by the slow electron oscillations as well as couple to longer wavelengths and lower energy radiation [59]. Also, in basic environments, -COO- groups cause more water to be attracted over nanoparticles. Since water has a lower refractive index than nanoparticles, light scattering of the nanoparticles is decreased. As a result, the absorption of the plasmon band is decreased at high pH [58]. Thus, variations in acidic or basic environments have minimal impact on water adsorption on the structure of GQDs/AgNPs and, consequently, on plasmon band absorption.

After confirming the stability of the nanoparticles under various pH, some structural analyses were performed on the treated GQDs/AgNPs in both acidic (pH = 3.12) and basic (pH = 11.13) environments (Figs. S1–S3). The results indicated that the structure of these nanoparticles remains unchanged under the different conditions tested.

Various parameters, such as the source of silver and GQDs in the prepared compound, nominated GQDs/AgNPs as a suitable catalyst for the reduction of nitro compounds 4. For this purpose, initially, the catalytic activity of the GQDs/AgNPs was investigated in the reduction of nitro derivatives. Next, to further examine the efficiency of this catalyst, the generality and activity of GQDs/AgNPs were studied in tandem Ugi reactions using nitro compound derivatives 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic properties of GQDs/AgNPs for nitro reduction
Catalytic properties of GQDs/AgNPs for the model nitro reduction reaction
ce:section.ce:para: Among the many applications of NaBH4 in various catalytic reactions [60], its use as a reducing agent in the nitro reduction reactions has been abundantly reported [61]. On the other hand, conversion of nitro compounds to corresponding amines is not possible using NaBH4 alone [62]. The AgNPs overcome this problem by facilitating electron transfer from NaBH4 ions (donor) to NBs ions (acceptor) and catalyzing the reaction [63]. The mechanism for catalytic conversion of NBs to aromatic amines using NaBH4 and AgNPs, which includes three main steps, was correctly recognized. First of all, the NBs are absorbed by the surface of AgNPs. In the second step, respective aromatic amines are created by an electron transport between NaBH4 and NPs. Finally, synthesized molecules are released from the surface of AgNPs to prepare the surface for a new catalytic cycle [64]. In this study, to investigate the catalytic performance of the GQDs/AgNPs, the reduction of NB 4a to aniline 5a in the presence of NaBH4 as a model reaction was studied by UV–vis spectrophotometric analyses in an aqueous medium at ambient temperature. For this purpose, 25 μL (10 mM) of NB 4a and 1 μg of catalyst were respectively added into a cell containing distilled water (1800 μL). As shown in Fig. 7 a, it is indicated that NB 4a and GQDs/AgNPs have a maximum absorption at 265 nm and 405 nm, respectively, in an aqueous medium. When a freshly prepared NaBH4 solution (200 μL, 10 mM) was added, a new peak at 230 nm with progressive increase in intensity was detected after 1 min. The progressive increase of the new peak and gradual decrease of the NB 4a peak prove the formation of aniline 5a without any side products. This process continued for 15 min and then stopped, indicating the end of the reduction reaction at high conversion (96 %).

To optimize the reaction conditions, the effect of GQDs/AgNPs concentration as a catalyst was checked in the reaction. The reaction did not proceed in the absence of the catalyst (Fig. 7c). When the amount of the catalyst was halved, the reaction time increased. This was despite the fact that 2 μg of GQDs/AgNPs was associated with negative effects in the reaction process. In the following, the effects of reducing agents in the reaction were investigated. For this purpose, at first, the reaction was tested without the reducing agent, but no product was obtained (Fig. 7b). Further studies showed that NaBH4 causes the progress of the reaction, in contrast to compounds such as ascorbic acid, hydrazine hydrate, and molecular hydrogen not leading to product production. According to these results and previous reports [65], an excess amount of NaBH4 (about 8 times more than NB 4a) was used in the reduction reaction. In the continuation of the reaction conditions optimization, the effects of temperature were investigated in the presence of optimized amounts of the catalyst and an excess amount of reducing agent. The reduction reaction of NB 4a was tested at different temperatures, but surprisingly, it was found that increasing the temperature does not have a favorable effect on the reaction process. As a result, 1 μg of the catalyst along with the excess amount of NaBH4 in distilled water at room temperature was selected as the optimized conditions for the reduction reaction of NB 4a (25 μL, 10 mM).

If the reaction kinetics is first-order, the rate constant of the reaction can be obtained from Fig. 7d. For this purpose, additional amounts of excess NaBH4 should be used so that its concentration is always considered the same during the reaction. Therefore, reaction kinetics can be described by a pseudo-first-order reaction (Eq. (1)), similar to previous reports [22]. As shown in Fig. 7d, the time-dependent of the ln (A
0 /A
t ) plot gives a good linear relationship with the correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9897, which is consistent with pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics. As a result, the observed rate constant (k
obs ) was calculated to be 0.0.0025 s−1. Also, the conversion of NB 4a to aniline 5a during optimization was calculated using Fig. 7e and its corresponding equation.

Considering that k
obs is independent of the amount of catalysts, normalized rate constants (k
nor) are used to more accurately compare the catalytic activity of GQDs/AgNPs with some other catalysts for the reduction of NB 4a or 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) (k
nor is calculated from k
obs /mg, where parameter mg is the total mass of the catalyst). As shown in Table 1 , the k
nor of GQDs/AgNPs (2500 s−1mg−1) is significantly superior to most silver-based catalysts. These investigations showed that GQDs/AgNPs have high catalytic activity, which may be a reason for their use in industry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic properties of GQDs/AgNPs for the reduction of nitro compounds derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: According to the attractive results of the model reaction, several NB derivatives 4 were reduced under general procedure (Table 2 ). All substrates, such as NBs 4 containing electron-donating and electron-withdrawing groups, were converted to the corresponding amines 5 in favorable yields (80–90 %). It is worth noting that the reduction of halo-substituted NBs 4 was successfully carried out without any halogenation (entries 4–6). Furthermore, the presence of several groups on the derivatives of NB 4 did not have an impressive consequence on the reaction, so compounds 4e and 4f were converted into their corresponding amines (5e and 5f) in 80 % and 82 % yields, respectively.

A comparison of the catalytic efficiency of GQDs/AgNPs in the reduction of NB with other various catalysts is presented in Table S1. The results indicate that GQDs/AgNPs exhibits satisfactory catalytic performance compared to some reported catalytic systems, considering comprehensive parameters including the amount of used catalyst, the reaction temperature, the reaction time, solvent and yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic properties of GQDs/AgNPs for the tandem Ugi reaction
ce:section.ce:para: Although peptides, as well as proteins, play a fundamental role in all physiological and biological activities, the low biostability and bioavailability of peptides prevent their widespread use as drugs [75]. To solve this problem, peptide-based peptidomimetics have emerged [76]. These compounds, which have functional groups in structures similar to active peptides, lead to better medicinal properties in some cases [77]. Various methods have been reported for the synthesis of peptidomimetics, all of which have shortcomings, including poor yields and several multiple stages [78,79]. In the meantime, using multicomponent reactions (MCRs) has been promoted as a solution to these problems [80]. In recent years, the Ugi reaction has attracted the attention of scientists as the most prominent isocyanide-based multicomponent reactions (I-MCRs) for synthesizing peptidomimetics [81]. Accordingly, the generality and activity of GQDs/AgNPs as efficient catalyst were checked in tandem Ugi reactions using NB derivatives 4. For this idea, a reaction between benzaldehyde 6 (1 mmol), 4-methylbenzoic acid 7a (1 mmol), cyclohexyl isocyanide 8a (1 mmol), and an excess amount of NB 4a (1.5 mmol) was performed under the reduction conditions. First, the NB 4a was added to the reaction medium containing catalyst, reductant, and distilled water. The other reactants were added after 10 min. At the end, the reaction was accomplished after 30 min in excellent yield (92 %) at room temperature. In this one-pot two-step reaction, the excess amount of NB 4a in step 1 is to create enough aniline 5a for step 2. Also, all the NaBH4 was consumed in step 1, and it will not disturb step 2. According to these favorable results, two derivatives of NB 4a and 4d (with the highest yield in the reduction reaction) and other reactants were utilized in the tandem Ugi reaction, and some of the peptidomimetics 9 were prepared. As shown in Table 3 , all target products 9 were synthesized in good to excellent yield.

Then, the catalytic activity of GQDs/AgNPs in the classical Ugi reaction was investigated to determine the role of the catalyst in the second step of the tandem Ugi reaction (Scheme 2 ). The reaction was performed in the absence and presence of the catalyst with low yield (after 12 h) [43] and high yield (after 30 min), respectively.

To gain a comprehensive insight into the reaction pathway, the proposed mechanism for the nitro reduction reaction and the tandem Ugi reaction utilizing GQDs/AgNPs catalyst are illustrated in Scheme 3 . Based on previous reported mechanism for the reduction of nitro compounds [82], it can be suggested the following steps for the catalytic conversion of nitro compounds 4 to the corresponding amines 5 in the presence of NaBH4. Initially, hydride ions (H−) are generated through the ionization of NaBH4 in aqueous medium, leading to the formation of silver hydride complexes (Ag-hydride) 10 via adsorption onto the catalyst surface. Subsequently, the nitro compound 4 approaches and adsorbs onto the surface of GQDs/AgNPs, where it undergoes reduction by the Ag-hydrides in a stepwise process. The first step involves the transfer of a H− to the oxygen atom of the nitro compound 4, resulting in the formation of a nitroso intermediate 11. This is followed by two consecutive hydride transfers that produce a hydroxylamine intermediate 13. Finally, the conversion of the intermediate 13 to the target product 5 occurs through the elimination of water. The final product 5 is then separated from the catalyst surface, allowing for the regeneration of active sites for subsequent reaction cycles.

Resulting amines from the reduction of nitro compounds can participate in the Ugi reaction cycle. In addition to facilitating the reduction of nitro compounds in the tandem Ugi reaction, GQDs/AgNPs also enhance the overall efficiency of the Ugi reaction. Previous studies have demonstrated that the presence of catalysts significantly improves two critical steps in the Ugi reaction: the activation of the aldehyde to form the imine and the activation of the imine for the subsequent addition of isocyanides [83]. Accordingly, it can be proposed the following mechanism for the GQDs/AgNPs-catalyzed Ugi reaction. First, aldehyde 6 approaches and adsorbs onto the catalyst surface, making it more susceptible to amine 5 attack. Then, an imine intermediate 14 is produced by the reaction between the activated aldehyde 6 and amine 5. The formed imine intermediate 14 is activated by GQDs/AgNPs, allowing isocyanide 8 to readily attack it to produce the nitrilium intermediate 15. Next, intermediate 15 is separated from the GQDs/AgNPs to regenerate the active sites of the catalyst. Following this, a proton exchange occurs between the desorbed intermediate 15′ and carboxylic acid 7, leading to the nucleophilic addition of the carboxylic acid anion 7′ to intermediate 15″ to form imidate intermediate 16. Finally, the target product 9 is achieved by a Mumm rearrangement.

In summary, we successfully synthesized a GQDs/AgNPs nano-catalyst using a green and inexpensive method. For this purpose, AgNPs were directly anchored on the surface of GQDs by a simple method. The resulting GQDs/AgNPs were characterized via several techniques and their stability under different pH was investigated using UV–vis spectroscopy. The catalytic activity of GQDs/AgNPs was examined through the NB reduction reaction as a model reaction. The reaction was carried out well in the presence of NaBH4 in water at room temperature and the process was monitored by UV–vis spectroscopy. Literature studies showed that the k
obs and the k
nor of the synthesized GQDs/AgNPs were acceptable compared to other silver-based catalysts used for the reduction reaction of nitro compounds. Furthermore, the reduction reaction of NB derivatives, the tandem Ugi reaction with nitro compounds, and the classical Ugi reaction were well catalyzed by this catalytic system.
